title: YouTube Video Search - System Design Interview
tagline: Design a text-to-video search system using text and visual embeddings, retrieval, fusion, and reranking.
problem: Build a video search system where users enter English text queries and receive relevant YouTube videos ranked using title, description, and visual content across a corpus of about 1 billion videos.

functional: Use textual metadata and visual content.
Log clicks and watch behavior for training.
nonFunctional: Low-latency serving over a billion videos.
No personalization in the base design.
requirements.designPrompt: Teach this as a multimodal search system: query understanding, text index, video embedding index, fusion, reranking, and evaluation.

capacity.label: Corpus size
capacity.value: 1B videos requires inverted indexes and/or vector retrieval rather than full scans.

capacity.label: Feature sources
capacity.value: Title and description text plus sampled visual frames or video embeddings.

capacity.label: Serving stages
capacity.value: Retrieve candidates, fuse signals, rerank, hydrate results.

capacity.label: Online metrics
capacity.value: Clicks, watch time, completion, and reformulation rate.

capacity.label: Core assumptions
capacity.note: About 1 billion searchable videos. Queries are English text only. Training data includes query-video interaction pairs. Serving uses precomputed video text and visual embeddings.

capacity.label: Design focus
capacity.note: Show why search uses retrieval followed by ranking. Then explain how visual evidence complements text metadata.

dataModel.name: video_text_embedding
dataModel.note: Text representation from title and description.

dataModel.name: video_visual_embedding
dataModel.note: Visual representation from frames or clips.

dataModel.name: query_log
dataModel.note: Search query and result interaction log.

steps.title: 1. Frame Video Search as Multimodal Ranking
steps.description: A text query should retrieve videos whose title, description, and visual content match the user intent. The serving stack needs retrieval first and ranking second.
steps.decisionPrompt: What signals define relevance for a text query over videos?

concepts.definition: Search that uses more than one data modality, such as text and video frames.
concepts.whyItMatters: It improves relevance when titles are incomplete or misleading.

concepts.definition: A broad stage that finds likely candidates quickly.
concepts.whyItMatters: It avoids scoring all billion videos.

concepts.definition: A precise stage that orders a smaller candidate set.
concepts.whyItMatters: It improves top results quality.

deepDives: No personalization means relevance is query-video fit, not viewer-specific preference.
bottlenecks: Overreliance on title text misses visually relevant videos.
talkingPoints: State the two-stage search pattern early.

steps.title: 2. Prepare Text and Video Training Data
steps.description: Text is normalized and tokenized. Videos are sampled into frames or clips. Query-video pairs from interactions become labels for training and evaluation.
steps.decisionPrompt: What preprocessing is required before models or indexes can use raw video data?

concepts.definition: Splitting text into model-readable units.
concepts.whyItMatters: It is required for query and metadata encoders.

concepts.definition: Selecting representative frames or clips from a video.
concepts.whyItMatters: It makes visual processing tractable.

concepts.definition: A training example connecting a query to a shown or clicked video.
concepts.whyItMatters: It teaches relevance.

deepDives: English-only scope simplifies tokenization and language detection.
bottlenecks: Sampling too many frames is expensive; too few misses visual evidence.

steps.title: 3. Encode Queries and Video Text
steps.description: A transformer text encoder converts queries, titles, and descriptions into embeddings or ranking features. The same normalization must be used in training and serving.
steps.decisionPrompt: How should the system represent text query intent?

concepts.definition: A neural model that produces contextual text representations.
concepts.whyItMatters: It captures semantics better than bag-of-words alone.

concepts.definition: Exact or near-exact term matching in an inverted index.
concepts.whyItMatters: It remains useful for names and rare terms.

concepts.definition: Matching by meaning through embeddings.
concepts.whyItMatters: It improves recall for paraphrases.

deepDives: A production search engine often combines lexical and semantic retrieval.
bottlenecks: Transformer inference latency for every query.
Rare exact terms can be diluted by pure semantic retrieval.
talkingPoints: Use hybrid text retrieval as a practical default.

steps.title: 4. Compute Visual Video Embeddings Offline
steps.description: Video processing workers sample frames or clips and produce visual embeddings. These are indexed so text queries can retrieve visually relevant videos through aligned embeddings or a learned fusion layer.
steps.decisionPrompt: How can visual content participate in text search without decoding videos during the request?

concepts.definition: A precomputed vector stored before serving.
concepts.whyItMatters: It removes expensive video processing from search requests.

concepts.definition: A vector summarizing a short video segment.
concepts.whyItMatters: It can capture actions that a single frame misses.

concepts.definition: Training text and visual embeddings into a comparable space.
concepts.whyItMatters: It lets text queries retrieve visual evidence.

deepDives: Index visual embeddings by video ID and model version so reprocessing is controlled.
bottlenecks: Backfilling 1B videos after a model upgrade is costly.
talkingPoints: Mention thumbnails alone may not capture full video content.

steps.title: 5. Fuse Text and Visual Retrieval Candidates
steps.description: The retrieval layer merges candidates from lexical text, semantic text, and visual indexes. Fusion normalizes scores and deduplicates video IDs before reranking.
steps.decisionPrompt: How should candidates from different retrieval systems be combined?

concepts.definition: Putting scores from different systems on comparable scales.
concepts.whyItMatters: It prevents one source from dominating unfairly.

concepts.definition: Merging and deduplicating results from several retrieval sources.
concepts.whyItMatters: It improves recall.

concepts.definition: A limit per retrieval source.
concepts.whyItMatters: It preserves diversity among candidate types.

deepDives: Fusion is where exact title matches and visual semantic matches become one candidate list.
bottlenecks: Scatter-gather across indexes can hurt latency.
Poor score normalization can bury good candidates.

steps.title: 6. Rerank and Serve Search Results
steps.description: The reranker uses query features, video text features, visual features, freshness, and engagement priors to order candidates. The API hydrates metadata and returns result pages.
steps.decisionPrompt: What features should the final ranking model use?

concepts.definition: Training a model to order results by relevance.
concepts.whyItMatters: It is the core search quality model.

concepts.definition: Fetching titles, thumbnails, and playback metadata for result IDs.
concepts.whyItMatters: It turns rankings into client-visible results.

concepts.definition: An opaque token for continuing a result set.
concepts.whyItMatters: It keeps paging stable.

flows.name: Search request
flows.note: The API retrieves candidates, reranks them, hydrates metadata, and logs the impression.

deepDives: Watch time is often a better online signal than click alone because search can reward clickbait thumbnails.
bottlenecks: Hydrating too many results adds latency.
Fresh videos need fast feature availability.
talkingPoints: Keep the result page stable across pagination.

steps.title: 7. Evaluate With Offline and Online Metrics
steps.description: Offline metrics like NDCG, MRR, precision@K, and recall@K test ranking quality. Online metrics such as watch time and completion rate validate real behavior.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do we decide whether a new search model should launch?

concepts.definition: Mean reciprocal rank of the first relevant result.
concepts.whyItMatters: It rewards putting a good answer very high.

concepts.definition: A graded ranking metric emphasizing top positions.
concepts.whyItMatters: It fits search result quality.

concepts.definition: An online experiment comparing model versions.
concepts.whyItMatters: It measures user impact.

deepDives: Search quality metrics need position awareness because users focus on top results.
bottlenecks: Offline labels can become stale as content changes.
talkingPoints: Include watch time and reformulation rate as online signals.

steps.title: 8. Refresh Indexes and Operate the System
steps.description: New and updated videos flow through text and visual indexing pipelines. Index versions are validated, activated, monitored, and rolled back if quality or latency regresses.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do new videos become searchable quickly and safely?

concepts.definition: Adding or updating documents without a full rebuild.
concepts.whyItMatters: It keeps search fresh.

concepts.definition: Publishing immutable index snapshots.
concepts.whyItMatters: It enables rollback.

concepts.definition: Monitoring latency, error rate, empty results, and quality signals.
concepts.whyItMatters: It catches search incidents quickly.

deepDives: Full rebuilds are needed for major model changes; incremental indexing handles freshness.
bottlenecks: Backlogs delay discoverability of new videos.
Bad index activation can affect all searches.
talkingPoints: Close with the full offline-to-online loop.

functional.how: Query normalization and text encoders feed lexical and semantic indexes for English query retrieval.
Frame and clip embeddings are computed offline and queried through a visual vector index at serving time.
Fusion combines candidates from multiple retrieval sources, then a learning-to-rank model orders hydrated results.
Text and visual indexers publish versioned updates, with metrics and rollback around index activation.

nonFunctional.how: Retrieval fanout, fusion, bounded reranking, and result hydration replace full-corpus scoring.
NDCG, MRR, clicks, watch time, and reformulation rate compare model versions before and during rollout.
Incremental reindexing lets new or updated videos become searchable without waiting for a full rebuild.

finalDesign.title: Final Design
finalDesign.description: The final design is a hybrid multimodal search system. Text indexing handles lexical and semantic query matches, visual indexing captures video content, fusion deduplicates candidates, a search ranker orders results using relevance and engagement features, and search logs train future query-video models.
finalDesign.decisionPrompt: Walk through indexing a new video and serving a text query that benefits from both title text and visual content.

concepts.definition: Lexical, semantic text, and visual retrieval contribute candidates.
concepts.whyItMatters: It improves recall over any single retrieval method.

concepts.definition: A layer that merges, normalizes, and deduplicates candidates from different indexes.
concepts.whyItMatters: It is where multimodal evidence becomes one candidate set.

concepts.definition: Queries, impressions, clicks, watch time, and reformulations feed evaluation and training.
concepts.whyItMatters: Search relevance must be measured against user outcomes.

options.title: Full Final Design
options.description: Complete architecture combining every major step from the interview. This is the default wrap-up view.

options.title: Text-only baseline
options.description: A useful baseline and fallback, but it fails the prompt requirement to use visual video content.

flows.name: New video indexing
flows.note: New videos update text indexes and visual vector indexes through separate but versioned pipelines.

flows.name: Search serving
flows.note: A query fans out to text and visual retrieval, then fusion and ranking produce one ordered result page.

messages.label: visual retrieve from aligned query embedding

deepDives: The visual index is not used by decoding videos at query time. It is served from precomputed frame or clip embeddings.
bottlenecks: Freshness backlog for newly uploaded videos.
Ranking can over-optimize clicks unless watch outcomes are included.
talkingPoints: Start with text search baseline, then justify visual content.
Tie launch decisions to NDCG, MRR, watch time, and reformulation rate.

followUps: How do you handle multilingual queries?
How do you avoid clickbait optimization?
How would you reindex 1B videos after a model upgrade?
